headline: Georgia's passing attack overwhelms LSU in lopsided Week 5 rout
body: TJ Giles carved up the LSU secondary with surgical precision, completing 32 of 44 passes for 353 yards and four touchdowns to earn Player of the Game honors. Georgia's offense moved at will throughout the afternoon, accumulating 471 total yards and never allowing LSU's defense to settle into any rhythm. The Tigers' secondary had no answer for the vertical spacing Georgia's receivers created, particularly Lamb, whose eight receptions for 103 yards established him as the primary target in the passing game.

LSU's offensive answer came in short bursts rather than sustained drives. While quarterback Nussmeier managed two touchdown passes, he operated in a perpetual game of catch-up, completing just 18 of 36 attempts for 151 yards. The Tigers' running game, anchored by Schaefer's 40 yards on four carries, never gained traction against a Georgia front that controlled the line of scrimmage. LSU's inability to sustain first downs kept their defense on the field and their offense permanently chasing points.

Georgia's defensive effort shouldn't be overlooked either. Hack's seven tackles anchored a unit that forced three-and-outs on multiple occasions and prevented LSU from establishing any momentum in the second half. The Tigers scored both their touchdowns in the opening stretch before the game's complexion shifted entirely. By the fourth quarter, this had become a showcase of Georgia's depth and execution rather than a competitive contest.
turningPoint: After Georgia opened a 21-7 lead midway through the second quarter, LSU never threatened again, unable to generate enough offensive consistency to stay in the game.

unsungHero.name: Hack
unsungHero.why: While Giles earned spotlight honors for his arm talent, Hack's seven tackles provided the defensive anchor that kept LSU's offense perpetually off-balance and prevented big plays when the Tigers had early momentum.
